measurements.measurement_reference: Second-floor window sill to lintel, ~17 courses in ~1.3m

brick_analysis.bond_notes: Standard half-lap running bond throughout main field. Buff/cream brick belt courses interrupt the red field at regular intervals creating polychromatic horizontal banding. No header courses visible in main field.

colour.description: Dark red-brown machine-pressed brick, very uniform colour. Institutional-grade brick with tight dimensional tolerance. Clean sharp arrises indicate high-quality pressed brick, consistent with 1878 municipal construction.
colour.polychromatic_notes: Buff/cream brick (#D4B87A) used extensively as horizontal belt courses, window surrounds, arch voussoirs, and decorative banding. Two-tone scheme is a defining feature of the building's Italianate civic design.

special_features.description: Large segmental brick arch over fire engine bay with buff brick voussoirs. Multi-ring arch approximately 3m span.
Buff/cream brick horizontal belt courses at regular intervals creating strong horizontal emphasis. Belt courses appear at floor levels, window sill levels, and as decorative string courses.
Corbelled brick cornice at eave line and elaborate corbelling on the clock/hose tower. Multiple stepped courses creating deep shadow lines.
Circular (oculus) windows with radiating brick/stone surrounds on the hose-drying tower.

special_features.location: above windows and as belt courses
special_features.description: Soldier courses in buff brick forming flat lintels and decorative bands.
Buff brick pilasters with decorative capitals flanking the main engine bay opening.

condition.spalling_locations: Minor spalling on some upper courses of tower
condition.notes: Heritage building in well-maintained condition. Some modern repointing visible but generally sympathetic. Original polychromatic brickwork intact. The close-up photo (145758658) shows machine-pressed brick with very clean arrises and minimal weathering.

texture_calibration.notes: Measurement from second-floor window. Building is a known heritage landmark (1878) so brick is institutional-grade pressed brick. Close-up photo confirms tight joints and uniform coursing.
